parties: Between Robert Clark, Applicant, and Dean Fox, in his capacity as Independent Chairperson of the Disciplinary Court of Matsqui Institution, Respondent
court: Federal Court Judgments: [1988] F.C.J. No. 650 Court File No. T-1122-88
body_html:
Federal Court of Canada - Trial Divsion
Vancouver, British Columbia
Collier J.

Heard: June 20, 1988
Judgment: June 24, 1988

Judicial review -- Penitentiaries -- Disciplinary hearings -- Assaults -- Self defence.

Peter Benning, for the Applicant.
Alan Favell, for the Respondent.

The Applicant is an inmate of Matsqui Institution. The Respondent, Fox, was the independent Chairperson of the Disciplinary Court of that institution. Clark had been charged under paragraph 39(b) of the Penitentiary Service Regulations,

"assaults or threatens to assault another person."

The Respondent found Clark, "guilty of the charge of assault."

He sentenced him to 20 days punitive dissociation.

This motion is to quash, by way of certiorari, the conviction decision.

The institution authorities considered Clark and another inmate, Ron Teneycke, had been in a scuffle or fight on March 7, 1988. The charge referred to was laid.

The evidence before the Disciplinary Court was to this effect: no institutional officer actually saw a fight or scuffle; Teneycke was treated at the institution hospital for some injuries; Clark was interviewed, on March 8, 1988, by an institution officer, Montgomery; that officer asked Clark if he had been in a fight or altercation the night before; Montgomery testified:

"he stated to me that he had, but it was over, it was done with, and there was no more problems with it."

I quote further from the transcript of the proceedings:

"CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes, Mr. Clark yes. I also had Clark remove his shirt and there were abrasions to both his arms around the elbow area and I think to the centre of his back on his knuckles and about a 1 1/2 inch to a 2 inch cut to his forehead, which was quite indicative of being in a fight of some sort. I phoned Preventive Security Officer, told him the information I had and they suggested that we put him in SCU pending an investigation and that was done.

CHAIRMAN: Do you have any questions for Mr. Montgomery?

CLARK: No, I don't.

CHAIRMAN: So you didn't actually see a fight?

CX5 MONTGOMERY: No, sir.

CHAIRMAN: But you read notes in the log book that other officers saw a fight.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Saw a scuffle.

CHAIRMAN: A scuffle.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes.

CHAIRMAN: And Mr. Clark admitted to you that he had been involved in a scuffle with Mr. Teneycke?

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes, he did, he didn't name the inmate.

CHAIRMAN: He didn't name the inmate.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: No.

CHAIRMAN: Did he say anything that would have lead you to believe that he was talking about the scuffle that was observed by the officers.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Not directly, no.

CHAIRMAN: Were you asking him about that scuffle, did you identify that time and place.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: I said last night did you have a scuffle, I didn't say where it was, who the other inmate was involved. I just asked if he had been in a scuffle with another inmate that night and I think I asked him around the approximate time.

CHAIRMAN: And he said yes?

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes, he said he was protecting himself.

CHAIRMAN: What else was said about it?

CX5 MONTOMERY: That was probably about it.

CHAIRMAN: That was then that you asked him to remove his shirt?

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes.

CHAIRMAN: Did he indicate whether it was over and done with.

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Yes, he did.

CHAIRMAN: Did he indicate why the scuffle had taken place?

CX5 MONTGOMERY: Basically all he said was to protect himself. I don't believe he gave me another reason that I can recall now.

CHAIRMAN: Other than protecting himself? CX5 MONTOMERY: Yes."

Both Clark and Teneycke denied being in a fight.

In arriving at his decision, the Chairperson said, among other things:

CHAIRMAN: So Officer Montgomery's evidence on the 23rd was to the effect that he was told by Nurse Gray that Teneycke had been assaulted and that he read in the log book that two (2) Officers, Hannason who hadn't been called because she didn't see it and Officer Parton who we did hear from and who said that she didn't see anything. Officer Montgomery also gave evidence that he asked you about a fight and you told him you had been in a fight with inmate Teneycke.

CLARK: I never said anything of the sort.

CHAIRMAN: That's Officer Montgomery's evidence, your evidence has been consistently that you weren't in a fight and we've heard from Mr. Teneycke that he wasn't in a fight and so the only evidence against you on this charge is the evidence of Officer Montgomery that you told him that you were in a fight. Do you recall the discussion that you had with Officer Montgomery?

CLARK: I don't ever remember saying at all that I was in a fight.

CHAIRMAN: Well, Mr. Clark, I'm satisfied on Mr. Montgomery's evidence which is supported by a report he written at 2 o'clock on the afternoon of the 8th tbe same day that he met with you, that you admitted to him that you had been in a fight.

CLARK: No.

CHAIRMAN: With Mr. Teneycke and on that basis I find you guilty of the charge of assault. Is there anything you wish to say about the factors that should be taken into consideration in determining what punishment is appropriate in the circumstances?"

"Assault" is not defined in the Penitentiary Service Regulations.

Counsel for the Applicant contended one should apply the meaning given by subsection 244 (1) of the Criminal Code:

"244.(1) A person commits an assault when

(a) without the consent of another person, he applies force intentionally to that other person, directly or indirectly;

(b) he attempts or threatens, by an act or gesture, to apply force to another person, if he has, or causes that other person to believe upon reasonable ground that he has, present ability to effect his purpose; or

(c) while openly wearing or carrying a weapon or an imitation thereof, he accosts or impedes another person or begs."

Further, it was said, the 'self defense' provisions, found in Section 34 of the Code would then become applicable; here, the only evidence before the Disciplinary Court was to the effect that Clark was only protecting himself in the fight or scuffle; on the evidence before the Chairperson, a finding of assault, beyond a reasonable doubt, could not be made.

Counsel for the Respondent contended the provisions of the Code should not be imported into the disciplinary offence set out in paragraph 39(b) of the Regulations. He cited certain decisions said to be in support of the general proposition. Counsel relied on dictionary meanings of assault. It was said that being in a fight, for whatever reason, was "assault" within the meaning of paragraph 39(b).

I do not find it necessary to decide whether or not the Criminal Code provisions should be imported into paragraph 39(b).

To my mind, the ordinary lay meaning of assault is an attack of some kind, intentionally, by one person on another. Merely protecting oneself or defending oneself by retaliatory measures does not, in my view, amount to assault by one inmate on another person. It may be, although it is not necessary to decide, unreasonable force in protecting or defending oneself might turn into assault.

If being in a fight and merely protecting or defending oneself amounts to an assault, than an impractical conclusion follows. The person attacked, to be not guilty, must offer no resistance or rebuttal; he must absorb the physical abuse, or try to escape. That is in accordance neither with human nature nor common sense.

The dictionary meanings of assault are, to my mind, helpful. The Compact Edition of the Oxford English Dictionary provides:

"N. an onset or rush upon anyone with hostile intent, an attack with blows or weapons"

"V. to make a violent hostile attack by physical means upon (a person or army, etc"

In the Living Webster Encyclopedic Dictionary of the English Language, the following appears:

"N. an attack or violent onset, physical or verbal"

"V. to fall upon with violence or with a hostile intention"

The Respondent, as I see it, equated participation in a fight as the committing of assault. On the evidence, the participation by Clark was to protect himself.

There was, in my opinion, an error in law in the decision reached. The mere fact the Applicant was in a fight does not mean he was guilty of assault as stated by the Respondent. To put it another way, the evidence before the Disciplinary Court could not support the conviction for a disciplinary offence based on paragraph 39(b) of the Regulations.

The motion is allowed with costs. The conviction is quashed. Thank you very much, gentlemen.
